coredata.dc:title: An intermolecular hydrogen-bond-induced quench-type Ru(dcbpy)3 2+/TPA electrochemiluminescence system by nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Xiaohong
Li, Libo
Luo, Lijun
Bi, Xiaoya
Zhao, Wanlin
Yan, Hui
Li, Xia
You, Tianyan
coredata.dc:description: Here, we show that nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) strongly inhibits the anodic electrochemiluminescence (ECL) signal of a tris(4,4′-dicarboxylic acid-2,2′-bipyridyl) ruthenium(II) (Ru(dcbpy)3 2+)/tripropylamine (TPA) aqueous system. To determine the ECL-quenching mechanism, we used photoluminescence spectroscopy, UV–Visible absorption spectroscopy and dynamic simulation technology. Quenching of the ECL signal of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+/TPA by NCQDs was predominantly attributed to the interaction between Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ and NCQDs rather than that between TPA and NCQDs. Specifically, when Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ and NCQDs were in aqueous solution together, the carboxyl (-COOH) groups of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ were in contact with oxygen- and nitrogen-containing groups on the surface of NCQDs and formed intermolecular hydrogen bonds. This process involved energy transfer from the excited-state Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ to the intermolecular hydrogen bonds, thus resulting in a decrease in the Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ ECL signal. On this basis, a quenching-type ECL sensor for the quantification of NCQDs was fabricated. The sensor had a wide linear range and an estimated detection limit of 0.0012 mg mL−1, as well as excellent stability and selectivity. Satisfactory recoveries of 97.0–99.5% were obtained using the ECL sensor to quantify NCQDs in tap water. NCQDs could potentially be used as a quenching probe of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ to construct various biosensors with widespread applications in the sensing field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ru(dcbpy)3 2+
Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
Electrochemiluminescence
Intermolecular hydrogen bonds

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Quenching effect of the Ru(dcbpy)3 2+/TPA system by NCQDs
Quenching effect of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ by NCQDs in the absence of TPA
Verification of the intermolecular hydrogen bonds between Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ and NCQDs
Performance of NCQDs based on the Ru(dcbpy)3 2+/TPA ECL system

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Project of Shandong Province Higher Educational Science and Technology Program
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The ECL behaviours of GCE in (a) PBS (0.1 M, pH 7.5), (b) PBS + 0.1 mM Ru(dcbpy)3 2+, (c) PBS + 0.1 mM Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ + 10 μM TPA and (d) PBS + 0.1 mM Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ + 10 μM TPA + 0.1 mg mL−1 NCQDs. (B) ECL, (C) UV–Vis absorption and (D) PL spectra of (a) 10 μM TPA, (b) 0.1 mg mL−1 NCQDs, and (c) 10 μM TPA + 0.1 mg mL−1 NCQDs. Scan rate, 0.1 V s−1.

(A) The ECL intensity of (a) PBS (0.1 M, pH 7.5) + 0.1 mM Ru(dcbpy)3 2+, (b) PBS + 0.1 mg mL−1 NCQDs and (c) PBS + 0.1 mM Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ + 0.1 mg mL−1 NCQDs. (B) PL spectra of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ and NCQDs and the ECL spectrum of their mixture. Conditions of the ECL spectrum: 2 mM Ru(dcbpy)3 2+; 0.01 mg mL−1 NCQDs. (C) UV–vis absorption and (D) PL spectra of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+, NCQDs and their mixture. Scan rate, 0.1 V s−1.

(A) The distance-simulation time curves of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ and NCQDs in different conditions (a: neutral solution, b: acidic solution, and c: 1: 1 ratio of –COOH: COO- on Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ and the NCQD surface). (B) ECL signal of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+/NCQDs and Ru(dcbpy)3 2+. (C) Quenching rate of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ upon the addition of NCQDs at different temperatures. (D) ECL signal of Ru(bpy)3 2+ identified as A (curve a) at different concentrations of NCQDs. Conditions: PBS, 0.1 M, pH 7.5; scan rate, 0.1 V s−1.

(A) ECL-time curves of the Ru(dcbpy)3 2+/TPA system at different concentrations of NCQDs from 0.002 to 0.2 mg mL−1. Inset of (A): photographs of the Ru(dcbpy)3 2+/TPA system at different concentrations of NCQDs under 365-nm UV light. (B) Calibration curve of IECL and logCNCQDs. (C) Selectivity and (D and E) Stability of the quench-type ECL sensor. (F) Repeatability of the quenching ECL sensor by six parallel measurements. Conditions: PBS, 0.1 M, pH 7.5; scan rate, 0.1 V s−1.
The possible quenching mechanism of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+/TPA ECL by NCQDs.
Quantification of different concentrations of NCQDs in tap water using the proposed sensor (n = 3) and UV–Vis method.

head.ce:title: An intermolecular hydrogen-bond-induced quench-type Ru(dcbpy)3 2+/TPA electrochemiluminescence system by nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Liaocheng University, Liaocheng, Shandong, 252059, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Liaocheng University, Liaocheng, Shandong, 252059, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Liaocheng University, Liaocheng, Shandong, 252059, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, we show that nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) strongly inhibits the anodic electrochemiluminescence (ECL) signal of a tris(4,4′-dicarboxylic acid-2,2′-bipyridyl) ruthenium(II) (Ru(dcbpy)3 2+)/tripropylamine (TPA) aqueous system. To determine the ECL-quenching mechanism, we used photoluminescence spectroscopy, UV–Visible absorption spectroscopy and dynamic simulation technology. Quenching of the ECL signal of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+/TPA by NCQDs was predominantly attributed to the interaction between Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ and NCQDs rather than that between TPA and NCQDs. Specifically, when Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ and NCQDs were in aqueous solution together, the carboxyl (-COOH) groups of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ were in contact with oxygen- and nitrogen-containing groups on the surface of NCQDs and formed intermolecular hydrogen bonds. This process involved energy transfer from the excited-state Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ to the intermolecular hydrogen bonds, thus resulting in a decrease in the Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ ECL signal. On this basis, a quenching-type ECL sensor for the quantification of NCQDs was fabricated. The sensor had a wide linear range and an estimated detection limit of 0.0012 mg mL−1, as well as excellent stability and selectivity. Satisfactory recoveries of 97.0–99.5% were obtained using the ECL sensor to quantify NCQDs in tap water. NCQDs could potentially be used as a quenching probe of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ to construct various biosensors with widespread applications in the sensing field.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) is a light-emitting phenomenon in which electrochemical intermediates form excited-state species via a reaction on the electrode surface and then luminescence occurs when the excited-state species returns to the ground state (Zhao et al., 2019; Hesari and Ding, 2016; Wu et al., 2020; Zhai et al., 2017). This method, integrating electrochemistry and spectroscopy, features good sensitivity, low background signal and a simple operation (Tang et al., 2013). Accordingly, ECL has been extensively applied for the construction of biosensors (Wang et al., 2020; Jia et al., 2020), biological imaging (Gao et al., 2020a, 2020b), defect detection on nanomaterial surfaces (Chen et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2016) and the dynamic analysis of electrochemical and luminescent properties (Hesari and Ding, 2017). Some of the most important ECL luminophores are tris(2,2′-bipyridyl)dichlororuthenium(II) (Ru(bpy)3 2+) and its derivatives, which are combined with tripropylamine (TPA) as a co-reactant (Huang et al., 2013a; Tang et al., 2013). Therefore, based on the strong ECL background signal of the Ru(bpy)3 2+/TPA system, several quenching-type ECL sensing platforms have been developed and corresponding quenching mechanisms have been proposed (Huang et al., 2013a, 2013b; Tang et al., 2013; McCall et al., 1999; Spehar et al., 2004; Cao et al., 2006; Huang et al., 2015). At present, the fundamental quenching mechanisms are approximately divided into four categories. 1) Benzoquinone-quenching mechanism: McCall et al. showed that the ECL signal of the Ru(bpy)3 2+/TPA system was strongly inhibited by benzoquinones and phenolic compounds (e.g. phenols, catechols and hydroquinones) because energy or electrons were transferred from the excited-state Ru(bpy)3 2+ to the corresponding benzoquinone and the oxidation products of phenolic compounds (McCall et al., 1999). 2) Electron-transfer quenching mechanism: For example, Cao et al. reported that ferrocenium (Fc+), the oxidative product of Fc, could effectively quench the ECL signal of Ru(bpy)3 2+ based on an intramolecular electron-transfer reaction from the excited-state Ru(bpy)3 2+ to Fc+ (Cao et al., 2006). 3) Resonance energy-transfer mechanism: In this quenching mechanism, when the excited-state Ru(bpy)3 2+ that served as a donor reaches a certain distance (1–10 nm) from the acceptor substance with spectral overlap, energy is transferred from the donor to the acceptor, thus leading to suppression of the Ru(bpy)3 2+ ECL (Huang et al., 2013b). 4) Carbon nanomaterials mechanism: Some carbon nanomaterials, including graphene oxide (GO) (Huang et al., 2015) or pristine multiwall carbon nanotubes (MWNTs) (Tang et al., 2013), as a kind of black-body analogue, suppress the ECL of Ru(bpy)3 2+ based on the energy transfer from excited-state Ru(bpy)3 2+ to the oxygen-containing groups on the surface of the GO or MWNT when they are close to each other.

Recently, as an emerging member of the carbon material family, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) composites with abundant oxygen-containing groups, featuring green preparation, good water-solubility, superior stability and good biocompatibility, have been applied in biosensing analysis (Long et al., 2014; Carrara et al., 2017; Xing et al., 2018; Li et al., 2017; Luo et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019; Li et al., 2015; Hu et al., 2019). However, in the Ru(bpy)3 2+-based ECL field, CQDs have different properties compared to carbon-based materials, such as MWNTs or GO, and can be used as nanomaterial-type co-reactants of Ru(bpy)3 2+ to enhance the ECL signal (Long et al., 2014; Carrara et al., 2017; Xing et al., 2018). In our preliminary work, several new types of carbon-based quantum dots, including nitrogen-doped CQDs (NCQDs) (Li et al., 2015), graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) (Luo et al., 2019; Luo et al., 2017; Luo et al., 2020; Li et al., 2021a) and poly(ethylenimine)-functionalised NCQDs (NCQDs@PEI) (Li et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2019) were developed as effective co-reactants of Ru(bpy)3 2+-based luminophores and applied to detect environmental pollutants. However, recently, we discovered that NCQDs could inhibit the ECL signal of the tris(4,4′-dicarboxylic acid-2,2′-bipyridyl)ruthenium(II) (Ru(dcbpy)3 2+)/TPA aqueous system with high-quenching efficiency. In this system, Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ replaces Ru(bpy)3 2+ as served the luminophore.

Herein, we systematically studied the quenching mechanism of the Ru(dcbpy)3 2+/TPA ECL system by NCQDs using ECL, photoluminescence (PL), UV–Visible (UV–Vis) absorption and dynamic simulation technologies. The results indicated that the formation of intermolecular hydrogen bonds between Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ and NCQDs quenched the ECL signal of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+. A quenching-type ECL sensor induced by intermolecular hydrogen bonds was constructed for the sensitive quantification of NCQDs and the analytical performance was determined.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching effect of the Ru(dcbpy)3 2+/TPA system by NCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The bare glassy carbon electrode (GCE) exhibits no apparent ECL signal in the PBS buffer solution (Fig. 1 A, curve a). When 0.1 mM Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ aqueous solution is added to the PBS buffer solution, an ECL signal with an intensity of 206 a.u. beginning at 0.95 V and peaking at 1.22 V is detected (Fig. 1A, curve b), which originates from the ECL of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+. As expected, the ECL signal is significantly enhanced (18-folds; 3718 a.u.) by the addition of 10 μM TPA (Fig. 1A, curve c), illustrating the co-reactant properties of TPA. Surprisingly, the introduction of 0.1 mg mL−1 NCQDs (Fig. S1) reduces the ECL signal of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+/TPA from 3718 a.u. to 238 a.u. (Fig. 1A, curve d), which is an interesting phenomenon that has not been reported before.

To studying the mechanism of this quenching behaviour, the interaction between 10 μM TPA and 0.1 mg mL−1 NCQDs was examined by ECL, UV–Vis absorbance and PL spectroscopy since it has been reported that CQDs can react with TPA to quench the ECL signal of the Ru(bpy)3 2+/TPA system (Yang et al., 2020). Neither pure 10 μM TPA (Fig. 1B, curve a) nor 0.1 mg mL−1 NCQDs (Fig. 1B, curve b) produce a macroscopic ECL signal and neither does their mixture (Fig. 1B, curve c), which illustrates that TPA and NCQDs do not emit light under these conditions. TPA has no obvious absorption peak in the UV–Vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 1C, curve a) while NCQDs exhibits two characteristic peaks at 336 and 404 nm (Fig. 1C, curve b), which are related to the π-π* conjugate structures of sp2 hybridised carbon (Li et al., 2015). After mixing, no direct chemical reaction occurs between TPA and NCQDs in the ground state since their absorption characteristics were preserved in the mixture (Fig. 1C, curve c) (Xing et al., 2018). Likewise, the PL spectrum indicates that there is no excited-state reaction between TPA and NCQDs because the NCQDs keeps their PL peak at 537 nm in the presence of TPA (Fig. 1D) (Long et al., 2014; Xing et al., 2018). The above results demonstrate that the quenching effect of the Ru(dcbpy)3 2+/TPA ECL system by NCQDs is not due to the interaction between TPA and NCQDs. These data are different from the previously reported quenching effect of the Ru(bpy)3 2+/TPA system by CQDs (Yang et al., 2020). Therefore, we speculate that the quenching behaviour of NCQDs on Ru(dcbpy)3 2+/TPA ECL may mainly originate from the interaction between Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ and NCQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching effect of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ by NCQDs in the absence of TPA
ce:section.ce:para: Investigation of the quenching effect of NCQDs on Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ in the absence of TPA was carried out to reveal the possible interaction between Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ and NCQDs. The GCE shows an obvious ECL signal in the PBS buffer solution containing 0.1 mM Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ (Fig. 2 A, curve a; 236 a.u.) but there is no ECL signal in the same solution containing only 0.1 mg mL−1 NCQDs (Fig. 1C, curve b). When NCQDs are introduced into the Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ solution, the ECL signal decreases from 236 a.u. to 50 a.u. (Fig. 1C, curve c), which indicates that NCQDs can indeed quench the ECL signal of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ in solution. This was verified that the ECL signal gradually decreases with increasing the NCQDs concentration from 0.05 to 0.5 mg mL−1 (Fig. S2).

The ECL spectrum of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+/NCQDs mixture is similar to the PL spectrum of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ (Fig. 2B) but different from that of NCQDs. Therefore, it can be inferred that the ECL signal of the Ru(dcbpy)3 2+/NCQDs system comes from Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ rather than from NCQDs (Li et al., 2015).

UV–Vis and PL spectra of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+, NCQDs and their mixture were further conducted. Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ shows two obvious absorption bands at 301 and 465 nm (Fig. 2C, curve a) which are assigning to intraligand π-π* transitions and metal-to-ligand charge-transfer, respectively (Zhu et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2017). NCQDs exhibits two characteristic peaks at 336 and 404 nm (Fig. 2C, curve b). Compared with pure Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ and NCQDs, the mixture solution exhibits two higher peaks at 432 and 465 nm (Fig. 2C, curve c), which may be related to surface interactions between Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ and NCQDs (Ryabenko et al., 2004; Fujimoto and Inuzuka, 1979). The maximum emission peaks of NCQDs and Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ are located at 537 (Fig. 2D, curve a) and 638 nm (Fig. 2D, curve b), respectively. After mixing, the maximum emission wavelengths are similar for NCQDs but blue-shifted by 10 nm for Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ (Fig. 2D, curve c) and this shift was accompanied by a decrease in luminescence intensity. According to previous reports, the weakening of PL intensity and the shift of peak positions suggests the reaction between Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ and NCQDs molecules under their excited state or ground state (Song and Ma, 2013; Yang et al., 2015).

Previously reports proposed that carboxyl-rich materials can form intramolecular or intermolecular hydrogen bonds with oxygen-containing CQDs, leading to reduced PL intensity and a shifted position (Song and Ma, 2013; Yang et al., 2015; Cayuela et al., 2015). Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ is rich in carboxyl (-COOH) groups and the surface of NCQDs contains a large number of –COOH, amino (-NH2) and hydroxyl (-OH) groups. On this basis, we speculate that intermolecular hydrogen bonds may be formed between Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ and NCQDs, which not only affect the PL intensity and position but also quench the ECL signal of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ by consuming the energy of the system (Dario and Fabrizia, 2000). Therefore, time-resolved PL spectroscopy was applied to distinguish between dynamic quenching and static quenching of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ by NCQDs (Bi et al., 2019; Nan et al., 2019). The lifetime of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ as the luminophore is decreased from 431 to 389 ns by the addition of NCQDs (Table S1; Fig. S3A), while the average lifetime of NCQDs does not change in the presence of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ (Fig. S3B). The results suggest that a dynamic quenching process based on intermolecular hydrogen bonds between the excited-state Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ and ground-state NCQDs may occur (Song and Ma, 2013; Leandri et al., 2019; Venkatesan et al., 2019).

Based on the above results and the literatures (Song and Ma, 2013; Chakraborty et al., 2020; Cser et al., 2002; Li et al., 2016; Fan and Yan, 2016), a possible quenching mechanism of the Ru(dcbpy)3 2+/TPA ECL system by NCQDs can be inferred (Scheme 1 ). Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ is electro-oxidized on the GCE surface to form the unstable Ru(dcbpy)3 3+ (Li et al., 2021b; Liu et al., 2021; Xue et al., 2020; Ye et al., 2019; Yuan et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2015). Simultaneously, the electrochemical oxidation product of TPA, the cationic radical (TPA·+), is also produced. The TPA·+ loses a proton to generate the reduced intermediate TPA· because of its short life of 0.2 ms. In the absence of NCQDs, this TPA· can effectively reduce Ru(dcbpy)3 3+ to the excited-state Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ (Ru(dcbpy)3 2+*). Next, Ru(dcbpy)3 2+* returns to the ground state, accompanied by a strong ECL emission. However, upon the addition of NCQDs, because of the contact between Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ and NCQDs, Ru(dcbpy)3 2+* can form intermolecular hydrogen bonds with ground-state NCQDs, which hinders the transition of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ from the excited state to the ground state and thereby reduces the signal intensity of the Ru(dcbpy)3 2+/TPA ECL system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Verification of the intermolecular hydrogen bonds between Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ and NCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Molecular dynamics simulations were performed to verify the intermolecular hydrogen bonds between Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ and NCQDs. For each status (a: neutral solution, b: acidic solution, and c: 1:1 ratio of –COOH: COO- on Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ and the NCQDs surface), the distances between the Ru atom and the NCQDs surface were calculated as a function of the simulation time (Fig. 3 A; Fig. S4). When the ratio of –COOH: COO- in Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ and NCQDs is 1:1, the calculated distances are maintained at ~1.0 nm, suggesting that the compound is tightly adsorbed onto the surface of the NCQDs. The intermolecular hydrogen bonds can be formed between the –COOH groups in Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ and the –COOH, –NH2 or –OH groups on the surface of the NCQDs (Fig. 3A, blue circle). The simulated result verifies theoretically our inference that when COO− groups are protonated to COOH groups, the electrostatic repulsion between Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ and NCQDs would be reduced, resulting in the adsorption of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ onto the surface through hydrogen bond interactions.

As pH is an important factor affecting hydrogen bonds (Yang et al., 2015), the influence of pH on the ECL signal of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ in the absence and presence of NCQDs was considered. The pH value mainly affects the deprotonation of –COOH in Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ on account of the good stability of NCQDs in the pH range 4–10.5 (Figs. S5A and S5B). Interestingly, the quenching of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ ECL by NCQDs is only effective at pH 6–9 (Fig. 3B), which may be attributed to the destruction of the intermolecular hydrogen bonds in strongly acidic or alkali solutions (Cui et al., 2004).

The effect of temperature on the quenching mechanism of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ by NCQDs was evaluated. The quenching rate of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ by NCQDs, defined as (I0–I)/I0 (I0 and I represent the ECL intensity of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ in the absence and presence of NCQDs, respectively) drops proportionately as the temperature increases from 20 °C to 40 °C (Fig. 3C) and there was a good linear relationship between quenching rate and temperature ((I0–I)/I0 = 1.292–0.028T (R2 = 0.9912)). The higher temperatures may destroy the intermolecular hydrogen bonds, leading to a decrease in the quenching efficiency (Ma and Lin, 2013; Mei et al., 2008).

To further understand the hydrogen-bond-induced quenching mechanism, we selected Ru(bpy)3 2+ as the luminophore to shield the –COOH of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ to attain different surface states. The ECL signal of 0.1 mM Ru(bpy)3 2+ (Fig. 3D, curve a; 100 a.u.) gradually increases from 332 a.u., 505 a.u. to 535 a.u. with increasing NCQDs concentration from 0.05, 0.1 to 0.5 mg mL−1 (curves b, c and d), which is in contrast to what occurs when Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ serves as the luminophore. Furthermore, the effect of NCQDs on Ru(bpy)3 2+/TPA ECL system was investigated, and an enhanced ECL signal is also obtained (Fig. S6). These data indicate that –COOH plays an important role in the formation of intermolecular hydrogen bonds. In summary, the above results show that there is an intermolecular interaction between Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ and NCQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of NCQDs based on the Ru(dcbpy)3 2+/TPA ECL system
ce:section.ce:para: Taking advantage of the high-efficiency quenching effect of NCQDs on the Ru(dcbpy)3 2+/TPA ECL system, the analytical performance of a quenched ECL sensor for NCQDs detection is investigated under optimal conditions (for experimental parameters, see Fig. S7). A continuously decreasing ECL signal from 7464 a.u. to 823 a.u. is observed with increasing NCQDs concentration (Fig. 4 A). Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ exhibits a strong red-yellow light and the image became gradually lighter under 365-nm UV light (inset in Fig. 4A) with increasing NCQDs concentration. The quenching effect of visible light further indicates the reliability of NCQDs quenching of the Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ light signal. Moreover, the as-developed quenched ECL sensor for NCQDs determination has a wide linear range of 0.002–0.2 mg mL−1 and an estimated detection limit of 0.0012 mg mL−1 (Gan et al., 2016). The linear regression equation is IECL = - 1638.3–3463.5 logCNCQDs (R2 = 0.9994; Fig. 4B). Compared with some previously reported Ru-QDs-based ECL system, the developed Ru(dcbpy)3 2+/TPA system exhibits the widest linear range for NCQDs detection (Table S2). More importantly, NCQDs can be potentially used as a novel quenching probe of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ to construct various biosensors with widespread applications in the sensing field.

The responsive time of the as-developed NCQDs sensor was evaluated to further assess the analytical performance (Fig. S8). The responsive time of the Ru(dcbpy)3 2+/TPA system decreases from 7.0 to 3.0 s upon the addition of 0.1 mg mL−1 NCQDs, which should be attributed to the situation that the formation of intermolecular hydrogen bonds between Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ and NCQDs can greatly decrease the diffusion time of luminous molecules (Dalapati and Biswas, 2017; Tseng et al., 2020; Yin et al., 2004; Zhang et al., 2018). Furthermore, the detection efficiency of the sensor was further investigated and its calculation method was presented in Formula S1-S4 (Li et al., 2019; Manghani and Ramsden, 2003; Zhang et al., 2016). As previous research reported, increasing the number of receptors covered on the sensor surface can improve the detection efficiency of the sensing platform (Zhang et al., 2014). In the as-developed ECL sensor, TPA, which is employed as excellent co-reactant, can react with unstable Ru(dcbpy)3 3+ to produce more Ru(dcbpy)3 2+*, thus increased number of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+* covered on the sensor surface and improved detection efficiency are obtained.

Selectivity of the quenching-type ECL sensor was investigated by recording the ECL signal with the addition of interfering substances (50 times concentration of NCQDs), including metal ions (Na+, K+) and inorganic ions (Cl−, SO4 2−). The addition of 1 mg mL−1 Na+, K+, Cl− or SO4 2− led to a decrease of only 0.94%, 0.39%, −0.23% and 0.41% in the ECL signal, respectively (Fig. 4C). However, 0.02 mg mL−1 NCQDs with 1/50-fold concentration of interfering substance led to an obvious decrease in the ECL signal with a quenching rate of 45%. Furthermore, the mixture of NCQDs and all of the other four ions show an ECL signal that was similar to pure NCQDs, illustrating that the ECL sensor can analyse NCQDs specifically.

The stability of the quenching-type sensor was evaluated by monitoring the ECL signal of the Ru(dcbpy)3 2+/TPA system without and with NCQDs for 30 cycles (Fig. 4D). The corresponding relative standard deviations (RSDs) are calculated to be 5.4% and 2.2%, respectively. It can be obviously observed that the stability of this sensor is greatly improved in the presence of NCQDs, which may be attributed to the fact that intermolecular hydrogen bonds between Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ and NCQDs can stabilize the ECL signal of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+/TPA system by reducing the energy loss induced by diffusion (Miao, 2008; Xie et al., 2019; Yin et al., 2004). The ECL signal of the sensor remained at 96.3% of the initial signal after storage at 4 °C for 7 days (Fig. 4E). The above results demonstrate that the quenched ECL sensor has good stability. Furthermore, the repeatability was investigated by recording the ECL intensity of six parallel ECL sensors and the RSD of the obtained ECL intensity was 0.86% (Fig. 4F), illustrating satisfactory repeatability of the Ru(bpy)3 2+/TPA system for NCQDs detection.

The practical use of the quenching-type ECL sensor was tested by quantifying NCQDs at different concentrations in tap water. The tap water was first filtered through a 0.22-μm cellulose membrane to remove impurities and stored at 4 °C until use. The recoveries from spiked tap water were between 97.0% and 99.5%, and the average recovery of the three NCQDs concentrations was 98.6% with RSDs of 2.59–6.49% (Table 1 ). Moreover, the accuracy of the developed quench-type ECL sensor was verified by the UV–Vis absorption method. The recoveries obtained by UV–Vis method were between 95.8% and 102% with the average recovery of 98.7%. It can be found that the recoveries of the developed ECL sensor is in good agreement with that of UV–Vis method, illustrating the comparable reliability of the developed ECL method in practical applications.

In summary, we report for the first time an effective and stable quenching of the Ru(dcbpy)3 2+/TPA ECL signal by NCQDs. After ECL, PL spectroscopy and UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy, we speculated that intermolecular hydrogen bonds could be formed between Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ and NCQDs, and energy transfer thereby occurred, leading to a decrease in the ECL of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+. This was confirmed by experimental verification and, on this basis, a novel quenching-type ECL sensing strategy is proposed. The sensor has a wide linear range and a low detection limit for NCQDs detection. NCQDs as a new quenching agent may broaden the application scope of Ru(dcbpy)3 2+-based ECL systems.

Xiaohong Liu: Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Libo Li: Methodology, Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Lijun Luo: Resources, Software. Xiaoya Bi: Resources, Software. Wanlin Zhao: Resources, Software. Hui Yan: Resources, Software. Xia Li: Resources, Software. Tianyan You: Supervision, Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 61801195, 22074055), Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (KYCX20_3036), the Innovation/Entrepreneurship Program of Jiangsu Province and the Priority Academic Program Development (PAPD) of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (No. PAPD-2018-87), and a Project of Shandong Province Higher Educational Science and Technology Program (KJ2018BZC043) and the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2019MB068).
